data.title: GOP uneasiness over Trump Iran threats grows as deadline ticks closer
data.description:
President Trump’s scathing threats against Iran are sending tremors across Capitol Hill, as anxiety mounts among some GOP lawmakers ahead of Trump’s 8 p.m. deadline.

Trump has intensified pressure on Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, a vital corridor that carries roughly 20% of the world’s oil supply. He said Iran would face “all Hell” if it failed to comply, including threats to strike its infrastructure, and issued an ominous threat on Tuesday that a “whole civilization will die tonight” unless “something revolutionary wonderful” occurs ahead of his deadline for Iran to strike a deal. 

The latest threats are deepening the growing sense of unease in corners of the GOP conference — and the MAGA movement more broadly — with some Republicans worrying about the U.S. becoming entangled in an even longer and costlier confrontation, some still in lockstep behind the president, and some hoping Trump’s words amount to bluster aimed at putting pressure on Tehran.

Rep. Nathaniel Moran (R-Texas) wrote on the social platform X that he does not support “the destruction of a ‘whole civilization.’”

“That is not who we are, and it is not consistent with the principles that have long guided America,” Moran wrote. “I have and will continue to support a strong national defense—one that is focused, disciplined, and firmly rooted in protecting the safety and security of the American people. But, how we protect the lives of the innocent is just as important as how we engage the enemy. America is great because America is good.”

Sen. Lisa Murkowski (R-Alaska) also criticized Trump’s latest threat, arguing on X that it “cannot be excused away as an attempt to gain leverage in negotiations with Iran.”

“This type of rhetoric is an affront to the ideals our nation has sought to uphold and promote around the world for nearly 250 years. It undermines our long-standing role as a global beacon of freedom and directly endangers Americans both abroad and at home,” she wrote. “The oppressive, terror-spreading regime of the Islamic Republic must be distinguished from the people and the civilization of Iran. Everyone involved—especially the President and Iran’s leaders—must de-escalate their unprecedented saber-rattling before it is too late.”

Sen. Ron Johnson (R-Wis.), a staunch Trump ally, said on the “John Solomon Reports” podcast Monday that he is “hoping and praying” that Trump’s threats are “really just bluster.”

“I do not want to see us start blowing up civilian infrastructure,” Johnson said. “I do not want to see that. We are not at war with the Iranian people. We are trying to liberate them.”

He added of the Iranian regime, “These people are — they’re fanatics. They’re apocalyptic. They may just be happy to try and usher in the end times. We need to prevent that.”

Rep. Kevin Kiley (I-Calif.), who recently switched from a Republican to an independent, wrote on X that the U.S. “does not destroy civilizations.”

“Nor do we threaten to do so as some sort of negotiating tactic. We should all desire a future of freedom, security, and prosperity for the people of Iran,” he wrote.

Outside Congress, some MAGA firebrands, such as former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) and conservative commentator Candace Owens, along with Democrats, have called for the 25th Amendment to be invoked against Trump.

The 25th Amendment says that the vice president and a majority of the Cabinet may vote to declare a president “unable to discharge the powers and the duties of his office” and give the vice president the duties of acting president. A dispute over the president’s capacity to perform the job would then be sent to Congress, which could concur by a two-thirds vote of both the House and the Senate.

“25TH AMENDMENT!!! Not a single bomb has dropped on America. We cannot kill an entire civilization. This is evil and madness,” Greene wrote on X.

But several Republicans have defended Trump’s remarks, calling them mere bluster and part of his usual negotiating style. 

“It is him negotiating Trump style. It is reckless words. But, I do want to see the regime buckle and make a true peace. I want to see the Persian civilization flourish but it cannot under the Ayatollahs’ yoke. Trump is using (bluster) to negotiate,” Rep. Don Bacon (R-Neb.) said. 

Rep. Mike Lawler (R-N.Y.) told CNN Tuesday morning that Trump is “not talking about obliterating innocent people.”

“We’re talking about taking decisive action against Iran’s energy and civilian infrastructure,” Lawler said. “That is what the president is talking about.”

The Iranian government said Monday that it rejected a “15-point” peace proposal from the U.S., calling it “unrealistic” and adding that it refuses “any negotiations conducted under the shadow of illegal sanctions, military threats, or coercion.” While Iran had sent over a 10-point response to end the war, according to Axios, Trump had argued that it wasn’t “good enough.”

Iran’s envoy to the United Nations, Amir-Saeid Iravani, said Tuesday that Tehran is willing to engage in “genuine” negotiations to end the conflict in the Middle East but will not accept a temporary ceasefire.

Democrats, meanwhile, have railed against the military operation. House Democrats are aiming to bring a war powers measure to the floor after lawmakers return from their two-week recess.

Reps. Thomas Massie (R-Ky.) and Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) brought a war powers resolution on Iran to the House floor less than a week after Trump joined Israeli leaders in launching massive strikes against Iran in late February. It failed by a vote of 212-219, with Rep. Warren Davidson (R-Ohio) being the only other Republican besides Massie to back it.

But three of the four Democrats to vote against the measure have said they would support it the next time around, and it is unclear how much GOP support the measure will receive in light of Trump’s escalation of the conflict.

House Minority Leader Hakeem Jeffries (D-N.Y.) said in a statement on Tuesday that the House must come back into session “immediately” and vote to “end this reckless war of choice,” and added that Republicans must put “patriotic duty over party loyalty.” 

Jeffries, though, hasn’t gone so far as to call for the 25th Amendment to remove Trump, as some members of his party have. Some Democrats have also called for impeachment proceedings against the president. It takes a majority vote of the House to impeach a president and a two-thirds vote in the Senate to convict and remove the president.

“This is why the 25th Amendment and impeachment were created. These aren’t policy differences. He is threatening crimes,” Rep. Melanie Stansbury (D-N.M.) said in a post. “It’s time to invoke the 25th. But we can’t do it alone—we need our Republican colleagues to do the right thing, for this country and for the world.”

Emily Brooks contributed.

data.title: Trump announces slate of Indiana endorsements ahead of May primary
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday announced a slate of endorsements for various Indiana state legislative races ahead of the state’s May primary. 

Although several of Trump’s endorsements, which were posted on his Truth Social account Tuesday afternoon, were previously announced, the president issued several new messages of support in the state’s House and Senate races.

Trump backed Republican Trevor De Vries in his bid to unseat incumbent state Sen. Dan Dernulc (R) in the state’s 1st Senate District as well as Republican Brian Schmutzler, who is seeking to beat state Sen. Linda Rogers (R). Both Dernulc and Rogers voted against the failed Trump-backed redistricting push in the state late last year.

“Dr. Brian Schmutzler has my Complete and Total Endorsement to be the next State Senator from Indiana’s 11th Senate District,” Trump said in one Truth Social post. “Unlike incumbent Linda Rogers, a ‘RINO’ LOSER who has failed the wonderful people of Indiana so badly, DR. BRIAN SCHMUTZLER IS A WINNER WHO WILL NEVER LET YOU DOWN!” 

A majority of Republican state senators voted against a GOP congressional map proposal in December that would have created a 9-0 edge for the party in Congress. Indiana Republicans’ currently hold a 7-2 majority in the lower chamber.

Trump’s endorsements against Dernulc and Rogers are not unexpected, however. The president and his allies warned that they would challenge any Republican lawmaker who voted against redistricting, as the White House pressed GOP-led states to redraw congressional maps ahead of the November midterms.

Democrats have responded to the move, passing a new map in California that is likely to net the party five seats, while Virginia Democrats are also looking to pass a new map later this month.  

The president on Tuesday also offered new endorsements for Republican state Reps. Martin Carbaugh, Ben Smaltz, Tony Isa, Bob Heaton, Bruce Borders, Beau Baird, Heath VanNatter, Jeff Thompson, Craig Snow and Jim Pressel. 

Trump’s endorsements come just weeks before the Hoosier State’s May 5 primary.

data.title: Trump's Iran rhetoric on wiping out civilization faces deep backlash
data.description:
President Trump’s extraordinary rhetoric threatening Iran’s civilization faced a severe backlash Tuesday amid conflicting signals over whether he was poised to make a deal with Tehran or intensify the U.S. bombing campaign.

Trump warned early Tuesday that “a whole civilization will die tonight” unless “something revolutionarily wonderful” occurs. Tuesday evening, there were signs that Trump could extend his 8 p.m. EDT deadline for Iran to take action. 

Pakistan’s prime minister asked Trump on Tuesday afternoon to extend the deadline, saying diplomatic efforts were ongoing. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said Trump was aware of the proposal and that a response to it would come.

Trump had sent shock waves around the world Tuesday with his warnings about destroying Iran’s civilization.  

But even then he was offering mixed messages, also writing “God Bless the Great People of Iran,” whom he previously promised to liberate.

The president has also repeatedly threatened to send Iran back to the “Stone Ages.”

The conflicting tone is raising questions about how serious Trump is about launching a full scale attack against a country of 93 million people if a deal is not reached, or if the language amounts to bluster.

“President Trump is making clear that he’s willing to impose substantial costs if those clinging to power in Tehran remain obstinate in trying to maintain the status quo,” said Cale Brown, former State Department principal deputy spokesperson during Trump’s first administration and chair of Polaris National Security.

“At the same time, he’s extended the hand of diplomacy to resolve the conflict without further violence,” Brown added. 

It’s unclear where things are headed by 8 p.m. EDT on Tuesday. In the hours before, Pakistan’s prime minister asked Trump to extend the deadline, saying diplomatic efforts were ongoing. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said Trump was aware of the proposal and that a response to it would come.

Meanwhile, Iran’s Foreign Affairs Ministry spokesman Esmaeil Baqaei hit back at what he called Trump’s “brute force” in a post on X following the president’s Truth Social post. 

“The power of a ‘CIVILIZED’ nation’s culture, logic, and faith in its righteous cause will undoubtedly prevail over the logic of brute force,” Baqaei wrote.

Tehran’s envoy to the United Nations Amir-Saeid Iravani warned the international body on Tuesday that Iran would take “immediate and proportionate” action if Trump follows through with his threats. 

The back-and-forth continues to cause upheaval for global energy markets.

Oil prices surged on Tuesday following Trump’s threats, with Brent crude, the global benchmark for oil, increasing by less than one percent. Meanwhile the U.S. benchmark, West Texas Intermediate crude, spiked 3 percent to $115 a barrel. 

U.S. embassies in the Middle East also reacted ahead of Trump’s deadline. The U.S. embassy in Bahrain urged employees to shelter in place, while the State Department raised the threat levels in Jordan, Oman, Saudi Arabia, and the United Arab Emirates to Level 2, possibly in anticipation of Iranian attacks at U.S. properties in those countries.

This is not the first time Trump has used strongman rhetoric in negotiations with a foreign adversary. In 2017, he threatened to unleash “fire and fury and frankly power, the likes of which this world has never seen before” against North Korea amid tensions over Pyongyang’s nuclear weapons development. 

Trump at that time did not act. But on Iran, experts say it’s important to take Trump literally and figuratively. 

“Those wringing their hands about how he’s conveying the message seem to have forgotten about President Trump’s penchant for speaking directly, even colorfully, unbound by the constraints of convention or precedent,” Brown said. 

“Whether you agree with his methods or not, it would be a mistake not to take him seriously,” he continued. 

But John Murphy, a communications professor at the University of Illinois who researches presidential war rhetoric, noted Trump’s history of using “hyperbole and exaggeration.” 

“He always talks much larger than he does, and he likes to exaggerate things to an enormous effect, and this is probably one of those things,” he said. 

Murphy said he could see Trump using former President Richard Nixon’s madman theory, a strategy employed by the Nixon administration to make the former president appear like a “madman” who would use nuclear weapons in order to get his adversaries to make concessions, specifically during the Vietnam War.

“I think that’s the same thing Donald Trump is trying to do today, and you saw JD Vance today in 

Hungary, also being very vague about all of the weapons at our disposal and that he’s willing to use them,” Murphy said.

Others are not taking Trump’s threat as seriously.

“We’re talking about taking decisive action against Iran’s energy and civilian infrastructure. That is what the president is talking about,” Rep. Mike Lawler (R-N.Y.) told CNN News Central. “He’s not talking about obliterating innocent people.”

Murphy questioned if Trump has “become the boy who cries wolf” because of the hyperbolic language he repeatedly uses.

“It is the tactic of people who care only about a short term outcome,” he said.

Other Republicans are, however, raising concerns over Trump’s rhetoric, warning of the consequences both nations would face if the president were to carry through with his threats. 

Sen. Ron Johnson (R-Wisc.) said in an interview on Monday that he is “hoping and paying that President Trump is, this really is bluster.”

“I do not want to see us start blowing up civilian infrastructure,” Johnson told the “John Solomon Reports” podcast. “I do not want to see that. We are not at war with the Iranian people. We are trying to liberate them.”

Texas GOP Rep. Nathaniel Moran also condemned Trump’s threat to destroy Iranian civilization. 

“I do not support the destruction of a ‘whole civilization.’ That is not who we are, and it is not consistent with the principles that have long guided America,” Moran said. 

Trump ally turned critic and former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene (R-Ga.) called Trump’s threat “evil and madness” and said the 25th Amendment, which allows the vice president and majority of the Cabinet, to deem the president unfit to serve, should be invoked.

“Not a single bomb has dropped on America. We cannot kill an entire civilization,” she wrote on the social media platform X. 

Far-right conspiracy theorist and former Trump ally Alex Jones also slammed Trump’s threats as “the exact definition of genocide” in a video reposting Greene’s post. 

Democrats also say they are taking the president’s threats seriously, saying it should not be viewed as a strategy in negotiations with Iran. 

“Threatening to commit acts of genocide is not a negotiating tactic,” said Brett Bruen, former White House director of global engagement under former President Obama and president of the D.C.-based consulting firm the Global Situation Room. 

“It creates the conditions that have led to some of the worst atrocities in human history,” he said.

data.title: Allegations Rep. Tony Gonzales sent explicit texts to second staffer spark calls for action
data.description:
New allegations that Rep. Tony Gonzales sent sexually explicit text messages to a campaign staffer marked the second time the Texas Republican has been accused of sexual advances toward an employee and are renewing calls from several Republicans for further action against the embattled congressman.

The San Antonio Express-News reported Monday that Gonzales had sent text messages to the political director for his 2020 campaign, repeatedly pressing her for nude photos while she demurred and describing how he wanted to have sex with her. The Express-News did not report the campaign staffer’s name but said that the two exchanged hundreds of text messages. The Hill has not independently verified the messages.

The staffer, who is a decade older than Gonzales, reportedly said that the relationship never became physical and that she was single at the time of his advances. She did not resign from her job at the time, but she later parted ways with Gonzales over political differences after he took office in January 2021, when she criticized him for voting for a commission to investigate the Jan. 6 Capitol attack.

The latest allegations follow a furor that erupted earlier this year when the Express-News reported that Gonzales had an affair with his district director, Regina Ann Santos-Aviles, who later died by suicide. Text messages said to be extracted from Santos-Aviles’s phone and provided by her widower to media outlets, including The Hill, appeared to show Gonzales soliciting sexual material from Santos-Aviles as she said he was going too far.

Gonzales admitted to the affair, which sparked an Ethics Committee investigation in the House and led to him effectively ending his political career. 

The Texas congressman ended his reelection bid last month after calls from a wave of Republican colleagues and House Republican leaders, but he said he intends to serve out the remainder of his term.

A couple of House Republicans, though, are itching to see further discipline in light of the new allegations.

Rep. Nancy Mace (R-S.C.), who has made her personal stories of facing sexual abuse core to her political identity, last month forced action on a resolution to publicly release all reports on file with the House Ethics Committee on investigations into members of Congress regarding allegations of sexual harassment or a sexual relationship with their staff. The House voted to refer that measure to the Ethics Committee, effectively killing it, with the committee warning it could create a chilling effect for victims and accusers.

“We haven’t forgotten the 357 members of Congress who voted to kill our resolution and keep congressional sexual harassment records buried, and you shouldn’t either when they ask for your vote,” Mace said Monday.

Rep. Anna Paulina Luna (R-Fla.) said she would support expelling Gonzales over the allegation.

“NO means NO. I’d vote to expel both him and Rep. Sheila Cherfilus-McCormick. Both need to go,” Luna posted Monday, in reference to the Democratic Florida congresswoman who an Ethics subcommittee recently found had violated 25 counts of Ethics violations related to allegations she used millions in improperly paid federal disaster funds to finance her campaign.

Republican leadership, though, has said that the Ethics panel should have the time to complete any investigation to offer Gonzales due process before any House action.

The House Ethics Committee officially opened an investigation into Gonzales the day after his primary to examine allegations that he violated the House’s code of conduct, which prohibits members from having sexual relationships with staff.

That rule does not technically apply to campaign staff who are not government employees. But it is possible that the Ethics panel could expand its investigation to scrutinize that relationship under other ethics rules and standards.

Gonzales had admitted in an interview with conservative talk show host Joe Pags that the affair with Santos-Aviles occurred, saying he “made a mistake” and had a “lapse in judgment.”

Gonzales’s office did not respond to a request for comment on the new allegations. He has not publicly addressed the new report.

data.title: Live results: Fuller, Harris vie for Taylor Greene Georgia House seat
data.description:
Republican Clayton Fuller and Democrat Shawn Harris are facing off Tuesday to fill former Rep. Marjorie Taylor Greene’s (R) seat in Georgia, after she resigned earlier this year.

Fuller, who is backed by President Trump, and Harris, a farmer and retired Army general, advanced to Tuesday’s runoff election in early March, after no candidate garnered at least half of the vote outright in the initial special election.

Polls close in Georgia at 7 p.m. EDT. Follow Decision Desk HQ’s live results here.

data.title: Live results: Wisconsin Supreme Court election underway
data.description:
Wisconsin voters will decide Tuesday between liberal candidate Chris Taylor and conservative contender Maria Lazar for a seat on the state’s Supreme Court.

Although the race has not garnered the same amount of national attention as last year’s race, the contest could further solidify the liberal majority on the state’s highest court for the years to come.

Both Taylor and Lazar currently serve on the Wisconsin Court of Appeals and are seeking to replace retiring conservative Justice Rebecca Bradley.

Polls close at 8 p.m. CDT.

Follow Decision Desk HQ’s live results here.

data.title: Live updates: Trump warns 'a whole civilization' could die in Iran; Pakistan asks for 2-week delay in deadline
data.description:
Around 12 hours before President Trump’s deadline for Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz and come to a deal, he warned the Islamic Republic on Tuesday morning in a post that a “whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again.”

Trump has given Iran until 8 p.m. EDT, telling reporters Monday during a news conference he would wipe out Iran’s bridges and power plants. On Tuesday afternoon, Pakistan’s prime minister asked for a two-week extension “to allow diplomacy to run its course.”

Meanwhile, the United States struck military targets on Iran’s Kharg Island overnight, a White House official said Tuesday.

Amid the escalation, some Republican lawmakers are growing wary about the Middle East military conflict, which has entered its sixth week. A wave of Democrats has called for Trump’s removal from office or impeachment.

On the campaign front, Wisconsin and Georgia are hosting a state Supreme Court and House election, respectively. Here’s what to watch for.

Vice President Vance, meanwhile, is in Hungary, where the party of Prime Minister Viktor Orbán is behind in the polls ahead of a Saturday election. Vance, at an event, took a call from Trump while on stage and lavished praise on Orbán.

Follow live below.

data.title: Gas prices concern Americans most amid Iran war: Pew survey
data.description:
The cost of the Iran war is weighing on Americans as joint operations from the U.S. and Israel stretch into a sixth week. 

Poll results released by Pew Research Center on Tuesday found that heightened gas prices resulting from the conflict in Iran are a concern for 7 in 10 Americans. 

The new survey found that 45 percent of people were “extremely” concerned, while 24 percent were “very” concerned about rising fuel costs. 

Nineteen percent of respondents were only “somewhat” worried about these hiked prices, while 9 percent said they were “not too” or “not at all” concerned. 

The average price of standard gas in the U.S. was up to $4.14 on Tuesday afternoon, a 27 percent increase from a year ago and a 21 percent increase from a month ago, according to AAA. 

Additionally, the global price of crude oil has skyrocketed as a result of supply chain shortages caused by the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz during retaliatory Iranian strikes in the Gulf states. 

The price of international benchmark Brent crude oil was up to $107 per barrel on Tuesday, and West Texas Intermediate crude oil was trading at $112 per barrel.

The Pentagon on Monday evening launched strikes on Kharg Island, which houses the majority of Iran’s crude oil reserves, driving up global oil rates. 

Markets are also anxiously waiting to see what President Trump will do once his Tuesday evening deadline passes.

Trump threatened on Tuesday morning that a “whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again” if Iran’s leadership doesn’t meet his administration’s demands by 8 p.m. EDT.

Respondents to the Pew survey indicated that their top concerns from the conflict were the possibility of a U.S. ground invasion in Iran, U.S. service member casualties and terrorist attacks on the homeland.

The poll included responses from a total of 3,507 U.S. adults from March 23-29 with a margin of error of approximately 1.9 percentage points.

data.title: Pakistan's PM asks Trump to extend Iran's 8 p.m. deadline
data.description:
Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif on Tuesday pressed President Trump to extend his 8 p.m. EDT deadline for Iran to make a deal, saying, “diplomatic efforts for peaceful settlement of the ongoing war in the Middle East are progressing steadily.”

He asked Trump for a two-week extension, “to allow diplomacy to run its course.”

“Pakistan, in all sincerity, requests the Iranian brothers to open [the] Strait of Hormuz for a corresponding period of two weeks as a goodwill gesture,” the prime minister said in a post to the social platform X Tuesday.

Sharif’s post follows a threat from Trump on Tuesday morning, when he said “a whole civilization will die tonight, never to be brought back again” unless “something revolutionarily wonderful” were to happen.

“We also urge all warring parties to observe a ceasefire everywhere for two weeks to allow diplomacy to achieve conclusive termination of war, in the interest of long-term peace and stability in the region,” Sharif said in the Tuesday post.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said that Trump had “been made aware of” Pakistan’s request, adding that “a response will come.”

Trump has been making increasingly intense threats against Iran over the Strait of Hormuz, a vital waterway for the oil industry that has been effectively closed since the start of the U.S.-Israeli conflict against Iran.

Mediators from Pakistan, Egypt and Turkey recently sent a proposal to Washington and Tehran with terms including a ceasefire lasting 45 days and the Strait of Hormuz reopening, The Associated Press reported, citing two officials in the Middle East.

On Tuesday, Iran’s envoy to the United Nations said that Tehran was inclined to take part in “genuine” negotiations to end the conflict, but would not go ahead with a temporary ceasefire.
